Cameroon 62 While filarial infection and disease are most frequently
Central African Republic 6 diagnosed among native residents of endemic regions, the risk of
Comoros 1 infection acquisition among travelers from nonendemic regions is
Congo 5 sizeable. Filarial species are found in tropical and sub-tropical
Cote d’Ivoire 3 regions of the world and, as travel to these regions becomes more
popular, filarial infection among nonendemic visitors becomes
Egypt 1
increasingly common as well. We describe here important
Ethiopia 9 epidemiologic characteristics of filarial infections acquired by
Gabon 7 world travelers from nonendemic regions as reported to the
Ghana 9 GeoSentinel network. While clinical presentation of filarial disease
Guinea 2 is known to differ between visitors to and natives of endemic
regions [3], our analysis also provides a quantitative assessment of
Liberia 11
filarial acquisition among travelers and helps describe the
Nigeria 10
differences in clinical presentation between those native to
Senegal 1 filaria-endemic regions and those traveling to those regions.
Sierra Leone 10 Filarial infections comprised 271 cases (0.62%) of all medical
Tanzania 1 conditions reported to the GeoSentinel network. O. volvulus was
Niger 2 responsible for the greatest number of filarial infections (n = 101),
followed by equal numbers (n = 68) of L. loa and W. bancrofti

significant.

Dominican Republic 2 (Table 2). There are numerous case reports and case series that
Haiti 6 describe durations of exposure as short as 10 days among filaria-
infected patients from nonendemic regions [5,9–12]. It is possible
that the lack of preventive measures such as insect repellent and
South East Asia 5 (2.2%)
bednets, as well as individuals close proximity to vector habitats,
Philippines 3 played a role in infection acquisition regardless of short durations
Vietnam 2 of exposure. Further, development of symptoms may also be
Oceania 4 (1.7%) dependent on the density of filarial larval inoculation as well as
Guam 1 individual innate immune responses [13].
